ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general omelette class (FOODON_03302051) but no specific Thai prawn omelette entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers omelette as a general egg dish. No specific prawn omelette code exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for prawn omelette or Thai egg dishes. Null applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Thai prawn omelette is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a single puffed omelette on a plate, often accompanied by steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, fried (USDA SR Legacy) and Shrimp, cooked (USDA SR Legacy) — composite estimate for Thai prawn omelette
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai prawn omelette exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424) and cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), weighted to reflect a typical Thai prawn omelette composition of approximately 70% egg and 30% prawn by weight, with added oil contributing to fat content. Values are cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional egg-dish data for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes and omelette analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai prawn omelette. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain omelette and egg-based dishes, which are consistently low-GI foods due to negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 3.6) / 100 = 1.15, rounded to 1.2. This is consistent with the low-GI, low-GL profile of egg-dominant dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 36–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a GI of approximately 32, prawn omelette has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as large portions of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and saturated fat from eggs and frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 200 g serving contains approximately 620 mg dietary cholesterol and 6.8 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate egg-based dish frequency and consider preparation with minimal oil to reduce saturated fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn omelette (Thai: ไข่เจียวกุ้ง, Khai Jiao Kung; Chinese: 虾仁煎蛋) is a popular Thai egg dish made by whisking whole eggs with fresh or frozen prawns, seasoning with fish sauce and white pepper, and pan-frying or shallow-deep-frying in hot vegetable oil until the omelette is puffed, golden, and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and custardy inside. The dish is a staple of Thai home cooking and street food, commonly served over or alongside steamed jasmine rice. In Singapore, it appears on Thai restaurant menus such as Sanook Kitchen. The texture contrasts a lightly crisp exterior with a tender, moist interior, and the flavour is savoury and umami-rich from the fish sauce and prawn. Nutritionally, prawn omelette is high in protein (approximately 13.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (13.2 g per 100 g, primarily from frying oil and egg yolk), and very low in carbohydrates (1.8 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 32 and a negligible glycemic load. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and choline from the egg yolk. The sodium content is moderate to high due to fish sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, it is a favourable protein-rich, low-GI option when portion-controlled and paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than large rice servings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Thai prawn omelette?
Is prawn omelette good for diabetics?
Is prawn omelette high in sodium?
How is Thai prawn omelette made?
What is the GI of prawn omelette?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai prawn omelette was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for fried egg and cooked shrimp, weighted by typical recipe composition. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database egg-dish data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy fried egg (FDC 173424) and cooked shrimp (FDC 175180) at approximately 70:30 egg-to-prawn ratio by weight, with additional oil contribution. No exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Sodium value reflects typical fish sauce seasoning; restaurant preparations may vary significantly. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database egg-dish data; no direct GI measurement for Thai prawn omelette was located in the peer-reviewed literature. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not independently verified from a primary regional source; these values should be treated as indicative. 5. The input food_group was 'Dairy and eggs'; this has been retained as the primary food group classification. 6. The cuisine tag 'Thai' from the input is supported by the restaurant context (Sanook Kitchen) and the dish's Thai culinary identity. 7. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient fields with numeric estimates are based on USDA SR Legacy component data. All numeric fields are populated with estimates rather than -1 where defensible component data exists.
